problem_statement: The paper addresses how increasing automation and automated decision aids (ADAs) in Security Operation Centers induce automation bias, complacency, and over-reliance among analysts, degrading their oversight of automated outputs. It proposes how to balance automation with human oversight so analysts remain the final decision maker.
magnitude_evidence: Alahmadi et al. (2022) '99% False Positives' study of SOC analysts; WannaCry estimated to affect 'up to 70 000 devices' in NHS hospitals
insufficiency_evidence: "automated systems can miss critical alerts" (Alahmadi et al., 2022); participants operating with incorrect automated decision aids perform worse than those not using automation (Metzger and Parasuraman, 2005); high-profile breaches (Target, WannaCry, Colonial Pipeline) where alerts were ignored due to complacency
solution_mechanisms: process-based automation (limit LOA in decision/action phases)
SOC analyst training on automated systems
key_constructs: Influence, Impact, and Mitigation of Automation Bias Model
notes: Domain is SOC alert triage/automation, not AI-generated code specifically; oversight-of-automation framing is analogous. Not empirically tested (author-acknowledged limitation).
